topic_name: Dockerfile & Docker Compose for Spring Boot

exercises.title: Why use a multi-stage Dockerfile?
exercises.instructions:
Single-stage Dockerfile uses eclipse-temurin:21-jdk-alpine (JDK image, ~440MB).
Multi-stage Dockerfile: stage 1 uses JDK, stage 2 uses eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine (JRE, ~190MB).
1. What is the final image size difference?
2. What is removed in the JRE-only image?
3. Why does smaller matter in production?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. JDK image: ~440MB → JRE image: ~190MB → savings: ~250MB (56% smaller)

2. JRE removes (vs JDK):
   - javac compiler (not needed at runtime)
   - jshell, jdeps, jlink tools
   - JDK headers and development tools
   - src.zip (Java source code)
   Total removed: development tools not needed to RUN a compiled app

3. Why smaller matters in production:
   - Faster image pulls: CI/CD to k8s node 190MB vs 440MB (especially in auto-scaling)
   - Less disk space: 50 containers × 250MB = 12.5GB saved per node
   - Smaller attack surface: javac removed = can't compile/execute new Java code if compromised
   - Registry costs: 250MB less stored per version × many versions
   - Container startup: less to unpack

test_cases.expected:
1. JDK image: ~440MB → JRE image: ~190MB → savings: ~250MB (56% smaller)

2. JRE removes (vs JDK):
   - javac compiler (not needed at runtime)
   - jshell, jdeps, jlink tools
   - JDK headers and development tools
   - src.zip (Java source code)
   Total removed: development tools not needed to RUN a compiled app

3. Why smaller matters in production:
   - Faster image pulls: CI/CD to k8s node 190MB vs 440MB (especially in auto-scaling)
   - Less disk space: 50 containers × 250MB = 12.5GB saved per node
   - Smaller attack surface: javac removed = can't compile/execute new Java code if compromised
   - Registry costs: 250MB less stored per version × many versions
   - Container startup: less to unpack

hints: JRE = Java Runtime Environment (run only)
JDK = JRE + development tools (compile + run)
Attack surface: fewer tools = fewer potential vulnerabilities

exercises.title: Complete the production Dockerfile
exercises.instructions: Fill in the JVM container flags in the ENTRYPOINT.
exercises.starter_code:
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine
USER razorpay
WORKDIR /app
COPY target/app.jar app.jar
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["java",
  "-XX:___RAMPercentage=75.0",
  "-XX:+___ContainerSupport",
  "-Djava.security.egd=file:/dev/./urandom",
  "-jar","app.jar"
]
exercises.expected_solution:
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine
USER razorpay
WORKDIR /app
COPY target/app.jar app.jar
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["java",
  "-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0",
  "-XX:+UseContainerSupport",
  "-Djava.security.egd=file:/dev/./urandom",
  "-jar","app.jar"
]

blanks.hint: MaxRAMPercentage: JVM heap = % of container memory

hints: MaxRAMPercentage: heap = 75% of container memory limit (not host RAM)
UseContainerSupport: reads cgroup limits, not /proc/meminfo

exercises.title: Write a minimal Spring Boot Dockerfile
exercises.instructions:
Write a simple (not yet multi-stage) Dockerfile for a Spring Boot app:
1. FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine
2. Non-root user 'appuser'
3. Copy JAR from target/
4. JVM container flags in ENTRYPOINT
5. EXPOSE 8080
exercises.starter_code: # Dockerfile for Spring Boot payment service
exercises.expected_solution:
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine

# Create non-root user (security best practice):
RUN addgroup --system appuser && \
    adduser --system --ingroup appuser appuser

USER appuser
WORKDIR /app

# Copy the built JAR:
COPY target/payment-service-*.jar app.jar

EXPOSE 8080

ENTRYPOINT ["java",
  "-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0",
  "-XX:+UseContainerSupport",
  "-Djava.security.egd=file:/dev/./urandom",
  "-jar","/app/app.jar"
]

# Build: docker build -t razorpay-payments .
# Run:   docker run -p 8080:8080 razorpay-payments
hints: addgroup + adduser: Alpine Linux syntax for creating users
USER appuser: all subsequent commands and the process run as appuser
Wildcard in COPY: COPY target/*.jar works if only one JAR is built

exercises.title: Multi-stage Dockerfile with layered jar
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete multi-stage Dockerfile:
1. Stage 1 (builder): eclipse-temurin:21-jdk-alpine, Maven build, layertools extract
2. Stage 2 (runtime): eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine, non-root user, 4 COPY commands
3. JVM flags in ENTRYPOINT
4. Comment explaining why each COPY is separate
exercises.starter_code: # Multi-stage Dockerfile with layered Spring Boot jar
exercises.expected_solution:
# Stage 1: Build the application (needs JDK)
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /build

# Copy Maven wrapper and pom first (cache dependency download):
COPY .mvn/ .mvn/
COPY mvnw pom.xml ./
RUN ./mvnw dependency:go-offline -q

# Build (dependencies already cached in layer above):
COPY src ./src
RUN ./mvnw clean package -DskipTests -q

# Extract layered jar:
RUN java -Djarmode=layertools -jar target/*.jar extract

# Stage 2: Runtime (JRE only — much smaller)
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine

RUN addgroup --system razorpay && \
    adduser --system --ingroup razorpay razorpay
USER razorpay
WORKDIR /app

# Copy layers separately for optimal Docker cache:
# Dependencies (Maven libs, ~150MB) — only changes when pom.xml changes:
COPY --from=builder /build/dependencies/ ./
# Spring Boot loader (framework code) — rarely changes:
COPY --from=builder /build/spring-boot-loader/ ./
# Snapshot dependencies — changes with SNAPSHOT dependency updates:
COPY --from=builder /build/snapshot-dependencies/ ./
# Application code (~100KB) — changes every build:
COPY --from=builder /build/application/ ./

EXPOSE 8080

ENTRYPOINT ["java",
  "-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0",
  "-XX:+UseContainerSupport",
  "-Djava.security.egd=file:/dev/./urandom",
  "org.springframework.boot.loader.launch.JarLauncher"
]
hints: dependency:go-offline: downloads all Maven deps before COPY src (cached separately)
4 COPY layers: each cached independently → only app layer invalidated on code change
JarLauncher: Spring Boot launcher class for unpacked layered jar

exercises.title: Docker Compose for Spring Boot + PostgreSQL
exercises.instructions:
Write docker-compose.yml:
1. app service: builds from Dockerfile, depends on postgres (healthy), env: SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=docker
2. postgres service: postgres:15-alpine, healthcheck: pg_isready
3. Named volume for postgres data
4. application-docker.yml with overridden datasource URL
exercises.expected_solution:
# docker-compose.yml
services:
  app:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
    ports:
      - '8080:8080'
    environment:
      SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE: docker
      SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/payments
      SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME: payments_user
      SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-changeme}
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy    # waits for pg_isready to pass
    healthcheck:
      test: ['CMD', 'wget', '-qO-', 'http://localhost:8080/actuator/health']
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 60s

  postgres:
    image: postgres:15-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: payments
      POSTGRES_USER: payments_user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-changeme}
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ['CMD-SHELL', 'pg_isready -U payments_user -d payments']
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

volumes:
  pgdata:

# application-docker.yml:
# spring:
#   datasource:
#     url: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/payments
#     username: payments_user
#     password: ${DB_PASSWORD:changeme}

# Commands:
# Start: docker compose up --build
# Stop:  docker compose down
# Reset: docker compose down -v  (removes volumes!)
hints: condition: service_healthy: waits for healthcheck, not just container start
pg_isready: Postgres built-in readiness check
Named volume: persists across compose down (not down -v)

exercises.title: Full Razorpay stack: app + Postgres + Redis + Zipkin
exercises.instructions:
Write docker-compose.yml with all 4 services:
1. app: Spring Boot with depends_on postgres (healthy) + redis (healthy)
2. postgres: postgres:15-alpine with healthcheck
3. redis: redis:7-alpine with healthcheck (redis-cli ping)
4. zipkin: openzipkin/zipkin, port 9411
5. All relevant env vars for app
exercises.expected_solution:
# docker-compose.yml — Razorpay payment service full stack
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports: ['8080:8080']
    environment:
      SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE: docker
      SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/payments
      SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME: payments_user
      SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-changeme}
      SPRING_DATA_REDIS_HOST: redis
      SPRING_DATA_REDIS_PORT: '6379'
      SPRING_ZIPKIN_BASE_URL: http://zipkin:9411
      MANAGEMENT_TRACING_SAMPLING_PROBABILITY: '1.0'
    depends_on:
      postgres: { condition: service_healthy }
      redis:    { condition: service_healthy }

  postgres:
    image: postgres:15-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: payments
      POSTGRES_USER: payments_user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD:-changeme}
    volumes: ['pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data']
    healthcheck:
      test: ['CMD-SHELL', 'pg_isready -U payments_user -d payments']
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    healthcheck:
      test: ['CMD', 'redis-cli', 'ping']
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5

  zipkin:
    image: openzipkin/zipkin
    ports: ['9411:9411']
    # No healthcheck needed — Zipkin is optional (app still works if down)

volumes:
  pgdata:

# After: docker compose up --build
# App:    http://localhost:8080
# Zipkin: http://localhost:9411
# DB:     localhost:5432 (if ports mapped)
hints: redis healthcheck: redis-cli ping returns PONG when ready
Zipkin: optional — don't make app depend on it for startup
Service names (postgres, redis, zipkin) = DNS names within Docker network

exercises.title: application-docker.yml Spring profile
exercises.instructions:
Write application-docker.yml that overrides:
1. datasource URL: uses 'postgres' hostname (Docker service name)
2. Redis host: 'redis'
3. Actuator: expose all endpoints (for Docker healthcheck)
4. Logging: structured JSON (production format)
5. Tracing: Zipkin at zipkin:9411
exercises.expected_solution:
# src/main/resources/application-docker.yml
# Activated when: SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=docker

spring:
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/payments   # Docker service name
    username: payments_user
    password: ${DB_PASSWORD:changeme}
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 20
      minimum-idle: 5
  
  data:
    redis:
      host: redis          # Docker service name
      port: 6379
  
  zipkin:
    base-url: http://zipkin:9411

management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: health,metrics,prometheus,info
  endpoint:
    health:
      show-details: always   # needed for depends_on healthcheck
  tracing:
    sampling:
      probability: 0.1       # 10% in docker (not 100% dev)

logging:
  # JSON format for log aggregation:
  pattern:
    console: '{"timestamp":"%d{ISO8601}","level":"%p","traceId":"%X{traceId}","message":"%msg"}%n'

server:
  port: 8080
hints: Docker service names = hostnames within Docker network
show-details: always: needed for wget healthcheck to get component status
profile-specific files: application-{profile}.yml auto-loaded by Spring Boot

exercises.title: Docker build optimization: .dockerignore and cache
exercises.instructions:
1. Write .dockerignore to exclude: target/, .git/, .idea/, *.log, node_modules/
2. Explain: why copying pom.xml and running dependency:go-offline BEFORE copying src is a cache optimization
3. Show the optimized Dockerfile order
4. How much time does this save in CI?
exercises.starter_code:
# .dockerignore file
# Optimized Dockerfile order (explain cache strategy)
exercises.expected_solution:
# .dockerignore — prevent unnecessary files from build context:
.git/
.gitignore
.idea/
.mvn/wrapper/maven-wrapper.jar
target/
*.log
*.md
Dockerfile*
docker-compose*
README*
node_modules/
.DS_Store

# WHY .dockerignore MATTERS:
# Without it: COPY . . sends the entire project (including target/ = 200MB JAR) to Docker daemon
# With it: only src/ and pom.xml sent (~2MB)
# docker build first compresses the build context — large contexts slow build start

# OPTIMIZED DOCKERFILE ORDER (maximizing cache hits):
# Stage: builder
# Step 1: Copy only pom.xml (rarely changes):
# COPY pom.xml .
# Step 2: Download ALL dependencies (cached as its own layer):
# RUN ./mvnw dependency:go-offline -q
# Step 3: Copy source code (changes every build):
# COPY src ./src
# Step 4: Compile (only when src changes):
# RUN ./mvnw package -DskipTests -q

# CACHE BENEFIT:
# Build 1 (first time): All 4 steps run → ~90 seconds
# Build 2 (code change only):
#   Step 1: pom.xml unchanged → CACHE HIT
#   Step 2: dependencies unchanged → CACHE HIT (saves ~60s!)
#   Step 3: src changed → cache MISS, runs COPY
#   Step 4: runs compile (~20s)
#   Total: ~25 seconds vs 90 seconds

# In CI (50 builds per day): 50 × (90-25)s = 54 minutes saved daily

public class DockerCacheDemo {
    public static void main(String[] a) {
        System.out.println("Order: pom → deps → src → compile → cache hits!");
    }
}
hints: .dockerignore: like .gitignore for Docker build context
pom.xml before src/: dep download cached when pom unchanged
Cache invalidation: any change to a file breaks cache for all subsequent layers

exercises.title: Container memory sizing for Spring Boot
exercises.instructions:
Spring Boot app needs:
- Heap: 512MB
- Metaspace: ~100MB
- Thread stacks: ~50MB
- JIT code cache: ~50MB

1. What container memory limit should you set?
2. What -XX:MaxRAMPercentage value for this app?
3. Write docker-compose mem_limit + reservation
4. What happens if heap exceeds container limit?
exercises.starter_code: # Container memory configuration for Spring Boot
exercises.expected_solution:
# Container memory sizing:

# JVM breakdown:
# Heap:         512MB (application objects)
# Metaspace:    ~100MB (class metadata)
# Thread stacks: ~50MB (1000 threads × ~512KB)
# JIT cache:     ~50MB (compiled native code)
# Direct memory: ~50MB (NIO, SSL)
# TOTAL JVM:    ~762MB

# Container limit = heap / MaxRAMPercentage × 100
# If heap = 512MB and MaxRAMPercentage = 75%:
# Container limit = 512 / 0.75 = 682MB → round up to 768MB (nice number)
# JVM overhead (25%): 768 × 0.25 = 192MB → covers non-heap

# docker-compose.yml:
services:
  app:
    image: razorpay-payments
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 768m       # hard limit — OOMKill if exceeded
        reservations:
          memory: 512m       # soft reservation — guaranteed allocation
    environment:
      - JAVA_OPTS=-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0 -XX:+UseContainerSupport

# WHAT HAPPENS IF HEAP EXCEEDS CONTAINER LIMIT:
# JVM tries to allocate beyond container memory limit
# Linux cgroup: kills the process (SIGKILL)
# Docker: container exits with OOMKilled status
# Kubernetes: reports OOMKilled in pod events
# Fix: increase container limit OR reduce MaxRAMPercentage

# RULE OF THUMB:
# Small services: 256m container → MaxRAMPercentage=60 (heap=154MB)
# Medium services: 512m container → MaxRAMPercentage=70 (heap=358MB)
# Large services: 1g container → MaxRAMPercentage=75 (heap=768MB)

public class MemorySizing { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("Container limit = heap / (MaxRAMPercentage/100)");
    System.out.println("OOMKilled: heap exceeded container limit");} }
hints: OOMKilled: container killed by Linux kernel for exceeding memory limit
mem_limit in compose: corresponds to -m flag in docker run
Reservations: requested at scheduling time; limits: enforced at runtime

exercises.title: What happens when depends_on has no healthcheck?
exercises.instructions:
```yaml
depends_on:
  - postgres  # no condition: service_healthy
```

Vs:
```yaml
depends_on:
  postgres:
    condition: service_healthy
```

1. What is the difference in startup behavior?
2. What error occurs without service_healthy?
3. How quickly does Postgres actually accept connections after container start?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. DIFFERENCE:
   depends_on without condition: service_healthy:
     Docker starts containers in dependency order, but waits only for CONTAINER START,
     not until the service inside is READY.
     Result: app starts when Postgres container starts, but Postgres init may take 3-5 seconds.

   depends_on with condition: service_healthy:
     Docker waits until the healthcheck (pg_isready) passes.
     Postgres healthcheck: pg_isready -U payments_user -d payments
     Only after this returns exit code 0 does Docker start the app container.

2. ERROR WITHOUT service_healthy:
   org.springframework.beans.factory.BeanCreationException:
   Could not obtain JDBC Connection; nested exception is:
   org.postgresql.util.PSQLException: Connection refused.
   The JDBC connection attempt fails because Postgres init (initdb, creating the
   'payments' database and 'payments_user') takes 3-5 seconds after container start.

3. Timeline:
   T=0:    docker compose up — Postgres container starts
   T=0.5s: container process running (postgres binary started)
   T=1-2s: initdb creates data directory
   T=3-4s: database 'payments' created, user created
   T=5s:   pg_isready returns exit 0 → healthcheck passes
   Without healthcheck: app starts at T=0.5s → connection refused
   With healthcheck: app starts at T=5s → connection succeeds

test_cases.expected:
1. DIFFERENCE:
   depends_on without condition: service_healthy:
     Docker starts containers in dependency order, but waits only for CONTAINER START,
     not until the service inside is READY.
     Result: app starts when Postgres container starts, but Postgres init may take 3-5 seconds.

   depends_on with condition: service_healthy:
     Docker waits until the healthcheck (pg_isready) passes.
     Postgres healthcheck: pg_isready -U payments_user -d payments
     Only after this returns exit code 0 does Docker start the app container.

2. ERROR WITHOUT service_healthy:
   org.springframework.beans.factory.BeanCreationException:
   Could not obtain JDBC Connection; nested exception is:
   org.postgresql.util.PSQLException: Connection refused.
   The JDBC connection attempt fails because Postgres init (initdb, creating the
   'payments' database and 'payments_user') takes 3-5 seconds after container start.

3. Timeline:
   T=0:    docker compose up — Postgres container starts
   T=0.5s: container process running (postgres binary started)
   T=1-2s: initdb creates data directory
   T=3-4s: database 'payments' created, user created
   T=5s:   pg_isready returns exit 0 → healthcheck passes
   Without healthcheck: app starts at T=0.5s → connection refused
   With healthcheck: app starts at T=5s → connection succeeds

hints: Container start ≠ service ready
pg_isready: Postgres CLI tool that checks if the server is accepting connections
initdb: Postgres database initialization takes a few seconds

exercises.title: Zerodha trading service Dockerfile + Compose
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Zerodha trading containerization:
1. Multi-stage Dockerfile: JDK builder + JRE runtime + non-root + layered jar
2. docker-compose.yml: trading-service + postgres + redis + prometheus
3. application-docker.yml: docker-specific config
4. .dockerignore
5. All healthchecks configured
exercises.expected_solution:
# Dockerfile
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /build
COPY pom.xml .
COPY .mvn .mvn
COPY mvnw .
RUN ./mvnw dependency:go-offline -q
COPY src ./src
RUN ./mvnw clean package -DskipTests -q
RUN java -Djarmode=layertools -jar target/*.jar extract

FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine
RUN addgroup --system zerodha && adduser --system --ingroup zerodha zerodha
USER zerodha
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /build/dependencies/ ./
COPY --from=builder /build/spring-boot-loader/ ./
COPY --from=builder /build/snapshot-dependencies/ ./
COPY --from=builder /build/application/ ./
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["java","-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0","-XX:+UseContainerSupport","-Djava.security.egd=file:/dev/./urandom","org.springframework.boot.loader.launch.JarLauncher"]

# .dockerignore
# target/
# .git/
# .idea/
# *.log

# docker-compose.yml
# services:
#   trading-service:
#     build: .
#     ports: ['8080:8080']
#     environment:
#       SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE: docker
#       SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/zerodha
#       SPRING_DATA_REDIS_HOST: redis
#     depends_on:
#       postgres: { condition: service_healthy }
#       redis:    { condition: service_healthy }
#   postgres:
#     image: postgres:15-alpine
#     environment: { POSTGRES_DB: zerodha, POSTGRES_USER: trading_user, POSTGRES_PASSWORD: zerodha123 }
#     volumes: ['pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data']
#     healthcheck: { test: ['CMD-SHELL','pg_isready -U trading_user'], interval: 10s, retries: 5 }
#   redis:
#     image: redis:7-alpine
#     healthcheck: { test: ['CMD','redis-cli','ping'], interval: 10s }
#   prometheus:
#     image: prom/prometheus
#     ports: ['9090:9090']
#     volumes: ['./prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml']
# volumes:
#   pgdata:

public class ZerodhaDockerConfig { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("Multi-stage Dockerfile + compose ready"); } }
hints: Same pattern as Razorpay — apply to Zerodha context
prometheus.yml volume: config file for Prometheus scrape targets
trading_user: consistent user name across compose and Spring config

exercises.title: Docker secrets for sensitive configuration
exercises.instructions:
Improve security by using Docker secrets (not environment variables) for:
1. Database password as a secret
2. Spring Boot reading from /run/secrets/db_password
3. docker-compose.yml with secrets block
4. How to create the secret file locally
5. Explain: why environment variables are less secure for secrets
exercises.expected_solution:
# docker-compose.yml with Docker secrets:
services:
  app:
    image: razorpay-payments
    environment:
      SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE: docker
      # DB_PASSWORD_FILE tells Spring to read password from file:
      SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD_FILE: /run/secrets/db_password
    secrets:
      - db_password
    # Spring Boot reads /run/secrets/db_password via spring.datasource.password
    # (requires spring.config.import=optional:file:/run/secrets/)

secrets:
  db_password:
    file: ./secrets/db_password.txt  # local dev: read from file
    # In Docker Swarm / Kubernetes: managed secret store

# Create secret file (local dev):
# mkdir -p secrets
# echo 'supersecretpassword' > secrets/db_password.txt
# echo 'secrets/' >> .gitignore  # NEVER commit secrets to Git!

# application-docker.yml for secrets:
# spring:
#   config:
#     import: optional:file:/run/secrets/
#   datasource:
#     password: ${db_password:}  # reads from imported secret file

# WHY SECRETS > ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES:
# Environment variables:
#   - Visible to all processes in the container
#   - Included in 'docker inspect' output
#   - Logged by some applications on startup
#   - Visible in /proc/1/environ
#
# Docker secrets:
#   - Mounted as files in /run/secrets/ (tmpfs — in memory, not on disk)
#   - Only accessible to containers explicitly given the secret
#   - Not visible in docker inspect
#   - Revocable and rotatable without redeploying

public class DockerSecretsDemo { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("Secrets: mounted as files, not environment variables"); } }
hints: secrets block in compose: mounts file at /run/secrets/secret_name
NEVER commit secret files to Git — add to .gitignore
Spring Boot: can read from files via spring.config.import

exercises.title: Docker health check for Spring Boot Actuator
exercises.instructions:
Configure a complete Docker healthcheck using Spring Boot Actuator:
1. Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK instruction using wget
2. docker-compose.yml healthcheck with start_period=60s
3. application.yml: custom health indicators
4. @SpringBootTest: verify health endpoint returns UP
5. Show: how depends_on + healthcheck enables zero-downtime restarts
hints: start_period=60s: Spring Boot needs time to start — don't check during startup
wget -qO-: quiet mode, output to stdout — grep for UP status
Kubernetes: uses liveness and readiness probes instead of Docker healthchecks

exercises.title: Debug: OOMKilled container — diagnose and fix
exercises.instructions:
Container is repeatedly OOMKilled after 5 minutes.
Symptoms: docker inspect shows OOMKilled=true, container memory limit=256m.

1. How to diagnose: what to check first?
2. How to find heap size without restarting app
3. Fix: correct MaxRAMPercentage for 256m limit
4. Show the corrected docker-compose entry
5. What is the maximum heap for a 256m container?
hints: JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS: JVM flags via environment variable (no Dockerfile change needed)
MaxMetaspaceSize: cap Metaspace to prevent unbounded growth
UseContainerSupport: must be set for JVM to read cgroup memory limits

exercises.title: Complete production Dockerfile + Compose — M21 T1 synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Razorpay payment service container setup:
1. Multi-stage Dockerfile with layered jar, non-root, JVM flags
2. docker-compose.yml: app + postgres + redis + zipkin + prometheus (all healthchecks)
3. application-docker.yml: all service URLs + actuator config
4. .dockerignore
5. Test: @SpringBootTest verifies health endpoint returns UP, counter registers correctly

All tests pass.
hints: WebTestClient: reactive test client, works for both WebFlux and MVC
actuator/prometheus: returns Prometheus text format (contains 'jvm_memory')
The Dockerfile/compose can be tested via integration test hitting /actuator/health
